text: Memorandum of Conveyance of Property at No. 14, High Street, Borough, to Mr Thomas Jones, of the same place, Draper. The title deeds are deposited at the London and Westminster Bank. The property is conveyed subject to a mortgage to the bank for £480, and Mr Jones is to have possession on Lady Day next. Dated this 20th day of January, 1859. Thomas Short, of 42, Holborn, Solicitor. Witness, John Denne, Clerk to Mr Short.

prompt_text: The apples want pruning before the buds break. I shall start with the russet at the south wall and work back towards the cider trees.
text: The russets must first be looked over, and all small spraygy shoots must be cut clean out, so as to leave the main branches at least six inches apart. The shoots must be shortened to about three buds, and the longest spurs must not be suffered to exceed nine inches. In doing this, care must be taken not to break off the spurs, which are very brittle. The next tree to be operated upon must be a nonpareil, and the pruning must be conducted in a similar manner, except that the spurs must not be shortened more than five buds. A golden pippin must next be treated in the same way

prompt_text: The apples want pruning before the buds break. I shall start with the russet at the south wall and work back towards the cider trees.
text: Prune the russets first, then clear away dead wood from the other trees, and finally thin out the branches where they are too thick. In doing this, let the leading shoots be left at full length, unless they cross each other, in which case shorten the interfering branches to about a foot in length. By no means cut in close to a bud, for the closer you prune the more spurs you will have, and the spurs are apt to produce small, deformed fruit. In short, thin the branches, but leave room for the free extension of the bearing wood. In pruning, keep the shoots perfectly clear of each other.

prompt_text: The cliff at Branscombe is plainly chalk above and a darker stratum beneath; the line between is sharp, and runs nearly level for half a mile.
text: The line of demarcation between chalk and green-sand at Black Down, in Dorsetshire, is equally distinct, though the two formations are there much more intimately blended together. The green-sand, which crops out along the northern side of the down, has a gentle slope towards the south, and the chalk, on the contrary, slopes in an opposite direction. text: Seville oranges are prepared for preserving in the same manner as directed for lemons (see LEMONS, page 459). This preserve is an excellent stomachic. It may be eaten at meals, like other preserves, but is most suitably taken early in the morning. In France, Seville oranges are used to make an agreeable liqueur, called Eau de Seville. The juice is first pressed out, and a syrup made of sugar and water; to every pint of which half a pint of the juice is added. The clear liquor is then distilled, and the spirit, which comes over, is sweetened to palate. The dose is from half

prompt_text: The Seville oranges are in. Six pound of fruit to ten pound of sugar; the peel sliced fine and the pith tied in muslin for the pectin.
text: Seville oranges are boiled in syrup, till soft, in proportion of ten pounds of sugar to six pounds of fruit. The syrup is then drained from them, and they are kept in it. The scum must be cleared off as it rises. The Seville oranges will keep good a twelve month. In France they candy the rind, and in England they preserve the pulp, separate from the rind.

prompt_text: The bitch whelped six in the small hours, four dogs and two bitches, all marked liver-and-white. She is settled with them and taking her milk.
text: Your bitch has had a fair litter, and you may reasonably expect to rear five of them. Feed plain food, giving milk and water first, then small portions of meat twice a day, and gradually increase the quantity. At six weeks old begin to give barley-meal and milk, in the proportion of two parts of the former to one of the latter, and continue the meal until the puppies are weaned, which should not be done until they are at least eight weeks old. Keep them clean, and let the kennel be dry and airy.
